does: This paper proposes Self-Taught Evaluators, an iterative self-improvement framework that trains reward models without human preference annotations. The approach starts with unlabeled instructions and iteratively generates contrasting model outputs, then trains an LLM-as-a-Judge to produce reasoning traces and final judgments using only synthetic data. The method bootstraps performance by retraining on the evaluator's own improved predictions at each iteration. Starting from Llama3-70B-Instruct at 75.4 on RewardBench, the approach achieves 88.3 accuracy (88.7 with majority voting), matching top supervised reward models while outperforming GPT-4, all without requiring any labeled preference data.
connects: This work addresses a fundamental bottleneck in modern language model development identified across recent scaling and alignment research: the expense and staleness of human preference data for training reward models. It builds on prior work in self-training and bootstrapping for machine learning, particularly the insight that models can improve by learning from their own iterative refinements. The approach extends LLM-as-a-Judge frameworks and reasoning trace methods by combining them with synthetic data generation, advancing recent trends in using synthetic examples for model improvement and removing reliance on human annotations that characterized earlier reward modeling work like those using labeled preference datasets.
enables: This work opens pathways toward reducing human annotation requirements in AI evaluation and alignment. By demonstrating that evaluators can reach strong performance through self-improvement alone, it suggests broader possibilities for bootstrapping capability across model development without constant human oversight. The approach could scale to new evaluation problems and domains where collecting preference annotations is prohibitively expensive or expertise is scarce. It also raises questions about self-improvement dynamics in evaluators and could inspire work on detecting and correcting error accumulation in bootstrapped systems, potentially informing more efficient evaluation-in-the-loop training paradigms for future model development.
